title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction service proposals for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Call to order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permit
CIP update
Street operation update
Utility operating update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Resolution 2026-014 transportation funding
Recycled base course production contract
Sanitary sewer discharge violation discussion
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue
Military Road construction agreement
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue road repairs by Integrity Grading for settlement issues, and manhole repair methods being evaluated with contractors.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Crews performed mailbox repairs, sign repairs, and curb line sweeping. Dewatering operations ran for approximately one week near Weston Avenue tributary to protect Crane Meadows from flooding. Discussion addressed need for permanent pump solutions. Two culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. Equipment maintenance included removing plow systems and servicing mowers.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary system, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into distribution but 53-54 million gallons treated at wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Investigation found sump pump discharges illegally connected to sanitary sewers. In-pipe Technologies will conduct an INI study to identify problem areas. Water main flushing notices going out with flushing starting next week.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has $6.8 million in cash reserves. PAS settlement funds of $1.44 million are earmarked for future treatment costs. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term funding solutions for state transportation. The village has decreased public works budgets while grants help one-time costs but don't cover recurring operational needs. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Motion to adopt resolution passed unanimously.

King Gravel submitted low bid of $4.23/ton for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at Ryan Street site, estimated at 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau's costs, representing about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of violations for illegal sump pump and footing drain discharges to sanitary sewer. Code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee directed staff to create enforcement language starting at $100/day for first offenses, distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Staff will provide cost analysis of treatment impacts at next meeting. Motion passed unanimously.

Agreement allows property owner at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect to water and sewer via long lateral until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, when they will pay assessment for main extension. Committee added requirement that reconstruction occur no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously.

Intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road project from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main relocation ($88,704) totals $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to approve agreement passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; MSA recommended due to lower cost and roundabout design involvement. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 and Clark Dietz $73,730; Clark Dietz recommended despite slightly higher cost due to having full-time experienced staff rather than intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Direct staff to create sanitary sewer discharge violation enforcement language with $100/day fine
Approve temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 utility costs
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelnik project and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project
Hold special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water treatment impacts at next meeting
Staff to provide update on responsive public service street lighting

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with minimal discussion.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive (Item 6, taken out of order)
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district (Item 5)
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items and staff discussion
Adjournment

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion included future expansion possibilities to the north and placement of an alternate storage shed. The motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt Storage Shed Inspection Program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and site drainage, and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT's was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and is closest to the village well. Commission agreed staff should reach out to that facility about compliance. The motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 Staff Approved Items and Building Permits
discussions.body: Both the certified survey maps/site plans and building permits for April 2026 were approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning Department Update and Future Items
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion of potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing was raised by a commissioner, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions. Electric vehicle charging stations were noted as a future agenda item for fall.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online participation. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the new police department to avoid unnecessary taxpayer expense, referencing previous relaxed standards for the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested that the salt storage shed inspection program include annual reporting on inspection results, noting some zoning complaints are years old.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for Board of Trustees approval with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection program results to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg regarding wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No major policy decisions were made as this was primarily an informational session to familiarize new committee members with departmental operations, budgets, and services.

agenda.item: Call to order and attendance (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg attending remotely)
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute, ordinance, resolution or policy
Educational presentations - Review of standing committee ordinances and jurisdiction
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veterans Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.item: Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with no opposition.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinances and jurisdiction
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under section 2.04 sub5 of county ordinances. He outlined reporting relationships with direct county departments (Veteran Services, Social Services, Health Department) and multi-county entities (North Central Health Care, ADRC of Central Wisconsin). Chair Boots noted the strategic plan development will be a focus this year, with objectives to be presented at upcoming meetings.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. She highlighted 1,200 licenses overseen, tick kit distribution through public health vending machines, the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new FTE position for homeless system response. Administrator Leonard noted the department budget is approximately $5 million with about half from tax levy and significant revenue from public charges for services and intergovernmental grants.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, noting the department has nearly 140 FTE with a 2026 budget over $22 million but only about $7 million from tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, explaining the board's feedback substantially changed and strengthened the program. He noted potential restructuring requests coming due to increased need in children's long-term support and child support units.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported the office now has 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 client interactions in 2025, up from 5,900. He noted $44 million in annual VA compensation comes to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. He highlighted housing stability work with Center for Veterans Issues and suicide prevention efforts, noting 7 of Marathon County's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional cooperation; Galindo explained state statute requires each county have a VSO but offices regularly collaborate.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He described services including meals on wheels, Medicare assistance, dementia care, and falls prevention. Ray noted serious financial challenges with nutrition costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remains flat. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meals program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data at future full board meeting
Strategic plan discussion to be added to upcoming agenda by Chair Boots
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)

title: Marathon County Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted Ringle and other communities. The board suspended its rules to consider the emergency tipping fee resolution, which reduces fees from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified storm debris.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Committee appointments by Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Announcement of Executive Committee election results
Consent agenda items C10-C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing supervisor leadership, and accommodating preferences. Over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous. Supervisor Nisa noted the system marked them absent without allowing a vote.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained nominating Supervisor Leur because she advocates for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on a motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal following severe weather that caused flooding and tornado damage, particularly in Ringle. The state emergency declaration allows waiving the $13.10 state fee, and Ringle is waiving its $2.33 fee. The proposal reduces Marathon County's portion from the standard $65/ton gate rate to the commercial rate of $52.37, resulting in approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely given that 44 homes need demolition and insurance adjusters haven't arrived for many. Administrator Leonard mentioned exploring partnership with the Community Foundation for unreimbursed costs. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules since the resolution didn't go through a standing committee, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously on a motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: No formal public comment period was held, but Supervisor Rosenberg requested special dispensation for two constituents to speak during the tipping fee discussion. Jessica Toasty spoke for three minutes, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving fees and asking Marathon County to do the same, mentioning one business has spent $40,000 on cleanup efforts and requesting a six-month fee waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke briefly on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting insurance adjusters haven't arrived for many of the approximately 44 affected homes and requesting extended fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large Executive Committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17th
Administrator to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy for handling similar situations within six months
Staff to track certified disaster debris and maintain documentation for potential state disaster aid

title: Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation - Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation - Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation - Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented a petition to rezone land from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented a petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make these conforming uses rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck raised concerns about fairness to others not in declared disaster areas. After debate, Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to only waive reconnect fees ($160 county fee for reconnecting existing septic systems to new structures), not other zoning or pouts fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor K and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an overview of the department's operations including the Bluebird Ridge landfill, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges requiring on-site treatment by 2032 per state law, and upcoming capital projects totaling millions of dollars. He noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about once weekly.

County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie. The department operates 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and manages city of Wausau parks and street trees. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only reconnect fees for tornado-damaged properties
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning approval to county board
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval to county board
Tom Lovelin retiring June 1st; Joe Tucker selected as new county forest administrator
CPZ to track costs and requests related to fee waiver program for future policy development

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, approving amendments to chapter 50 nuisances and an e-bike ordinance (with added emoto restrictions), recommending fire inspections for mobile food vendors, and authorizing a raze order for an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations but deferred action pending staff research.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Unfinished business - Proposed amendments to chapter 50 nuisances
E-bicycle ordinance amendments in chapter 82
Overnight parking regulations discussion
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze request for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Jim Senalt raised concerns about the new farmers market location at a village park, stating parking vehicles on grass violates village code and ADA-compliant handicapped parking should be on asphalt, not grass. He questioned the new fire inspection requirement for food trucks and noted some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.

Code Enforcement reported 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer. Fire/EMS had no additions. Reports were acknowledged unanimously on motion by Chwanka, seconded by Olsen.

The committee completed review of nuisance ordinance amendments, including switching tree limb height requirements to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpel, to recommend approval to the board passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion on regulating e-bikes, e-motos, and electric scooters. Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting e-motos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee debated whether to also restrict electric scooters used by children. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with added language prohibiting e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (typically 4-5 hours). Public Works Director Michael suggested either eliminating overnight parking restrictions entirely or signing specific problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred maintaining overnight restrictions for snow removal efficiency. Committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific problem areas for the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest parking lot. Staff estimated $2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system. Committee discussed church traffic patterns and safety. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously.

Fire Chief explained new regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer. One inspection sticker is valid across all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to maintain their own system. Chief noted a recent inspection failed due to improper propane piping and unsecured LP containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Kimpel, to approve ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing corrections needed for duplexes and out-of-sequence addresses on Delicowski Street, Ross Avenue, and Radkey Avenue. Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments with nine separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval.

Structure has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and posted since 2021. Fire department previously declined to do a training burn due to unsafe conditions. Owners are current on taxes and had previously housed chickens inside the structure. Demolition costs will be added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpel, to authorize raze order passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment raising multiple concerns: the new farmers market location will damage park grass, ADA parking should be on asphalt not grass, questioned the need for new food truck fire inspections, noted ambiguity in mobile food vendor ordinance language, and criticized slow progress on long-standing code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for board approval
E-bicycle ordinance with e-moto prohibition recommended for board approval
Public Works Director to identify specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions for July meeting
Public Works Director Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing; if they agree to pay, recommend board approval
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for E911 compliance recommended for board approval
Raze order authorized for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Parks Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Parks and Recreation Committee was scheduled to meet on May 26, 2026 to receive updates on Kennedy Park and the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course, and to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center, graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail, and kayak launch signage. The meeting included several community recreation facility matters relevant to Weston residents.

agenda.item: Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 27, 2026
Public Comments
Written Comments
Kennedy Update
Yellowbank Disc Golf Course Update
Discussion and possible action Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Discussion and possible action of Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Discussion and possible action Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

discussions.body: Director Osterbrink was scheduled to provide an update on Kennedy Park developments. Based on previous meeting minutes, this was expected to include information on the capital campaign, grants, and ongoing renovation work.
The committee was set to receive an update on the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course. This was expected to cover current status and any developments related to the facility.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to discuss and potentially take action on holding a Dog Day event at the Aquatic Center. This type of event typically allows dogs to swim at the pool at the end of the summer season.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider how to address graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail. Discussion was scheduled to potentially include response measures or prevention strategies.
The committee was set to discuss and potentially approve signage for the Yellowbanks Kayak Launch. This follows previous discussions about the launch facility and its amenities.

publicComment: Public Comments and Written Comments were both listed as agenda items.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 27, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Expected to consider action regarding graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Expected to consider action on Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Village of Weston Board of Trustees meeting was scheduled to address a significant increase to park and recreation impact fees through a public hearing, along with numerous license renewals, multiple ordinance amendments, and several infrastructure construction projects. The meeting also included the Board of Review being adjourned to a future date and consideration of a new police department and municipal court facility site plan.

agenda.item: Public Hearing and Consideration of Ordinance No. 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 to Amend Park and Recreation Impact Fees
Approval of April 13, 2026, Special Board of Trustees Meeting Minutes
Approval of April 21, 2026, Board of Trustees Meeting Minutes
Acknowledge Reports/Minutes from Boards, Committees, Commissions
Acknowledge Reports from Departments
Work Product Transmittals including April Building Permits, Budget Status, Code Enforcement Report, and Water Utility PSC Annual Report
Consent Agenda including Vouchers, Adult Establishment License, Weights and Measures Licenses, Cigarette/Tobacco/Vaping Licenses, Lodging License, Liquor Licenses, and Salvage License renewals
Ordinance No. 26-008: Amending Mobile Food Vendors License
Ordinance No. 26-009: Amending Standing Committees
Ordinance No. 26-011: Amending Chapter 50 Nuisances
Ordinance No. 26-012: Amending Bicycles regulations
Resolution 2026-014: Support for Long-Term Transportation Fund Solution
Amendments to Remote Meeting Attendance Policy
President's Appointments to Committees and/or Commissions
Approval of Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License for Schofield Coin & Hobby LLC
Compliance Assurance Plan (CAP) Policy
Feedback on Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
Address Corrections for E911 Compliance
Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
CliftonStrengths assessments and training for Board of Trustees
Amendments to Employee Personnel Policies regarding Clothing/Equipment Allowance and Appearance
Recycled Asphalt Base Course Production Quotes
Sanitary Sewer Discharge Violation Fees
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement for 2230 Radtke Avenue
Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
Construction Services Proposals for Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project
Construction Services Proposals for Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Public Hearing
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. The proposed changes would raise single-family fees from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the Village's 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: The Board was set to review and consider action on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. This represents a significant public safety infrastructure project for the Village.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was expected to discuss feedback regarding the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and recommended follow-up actions. This item was designated for discussion only, with no formal action scheduled.

The Board was scheduled to consider a resolution expressing support for a long-term, sustainable funding solution to the State's Transportation Fund. This resolution would position the Village in ongoing state-level discussions about transportation infrastructure funding.
The Board was set to consider an intergovernmental agreement for Military Road construction, which would formalize cooperation between governmental entities on this roadway project.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for a property at 4104 Shorey Avenue, which would require demolition of a structure deemed unfit for habitation or use.

discussions.item: Mobile Food Vendors License Ordinance Amendment
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider Ordinance No. 26-008, which would amend Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding mobile food vendor licensing requirements.

The Board was set to consider renewal of numerous business licenses for the 2026-2027 licensing term, including adult establishment, weights and measures, cigarette/tobacco/vaping, lodging, various liquor licenses, and salvage licenses.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda, allowing any person to address the Board for up to three minutes regarding non-agenda items. Additionally, a formal public comment period was scheduled as part of the public hearing on park and recreation impact fees.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on Ordinance No. 26-010 amending park and recreation impact fees
Scheduled to vote on Resolution 2026-015 amending the Village fee schedule
Expected to consider approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Scheduled to vote on consent agenda including vouchers and multiple license renewals
Expected to consider four ordinance amendments regarding mobile food vendors, standing committees, nuisances, and bicycle regulations
Scheduled to vote on Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation fund solutions
Expected to consider site plan approval for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
Scheduled to consider intergovernmental agreement for Military Road construction
Expected to consider construction services proposals for Alderson/Jelinek Intersection and Bloedel Ave Reconstruction projects
Scheduled to consider raze order request for 4104 Shorey Avenue

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Board of Trustees was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026, to conduct a public hearing on proposed park and recreation impact fee increases, consider multiple ordinance amendments affecting mobile food vendors, committee structures, nuisances, and bicycle regulations, and address various infrastructure projects including the Mountain Bay Metro Police Department facility and road construction agreements.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Public Hearing on Ordinance No. 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 to Amend Park and Recreation Impact Fees
Approval of previous meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed significant increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. Single-family fees were set to increase from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

discussions.item: Ordinance No. 26-008 Mobile Food Vendors License
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider amendments to Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding mobile food vendor licensing requirements. The specific changes to the licensing framework were set for trustee review and potential action.
The Board was scheduled to review proposed amendments to the administrative code regarding the structure and organization of standing committees within Village government.
The Board was set to consider amendments to Chapter 50 of the municipal code governing nuisance regulations within the Village.
The Board was expected to review proposed changes to traffic and vehicle regulations specifically related to bicycle use under Article III of Chapter 82.
The Board was scheduled to consider a resolution expressing support for long-term, sustainable funding solutions to address Wisconsin's transportation fund challenges at the state level.

As unfinished business, the Board was set to continue discussion on proposed amendments to Section 1.08 of the Elected and Appointed Village Officials Handbook regarding policies for remote participation in meetings.

discussions.item: Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department Facility
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to review and potentially act on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, representing a significant public safety infrastructure project.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was set to discuss feedback regarding the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and consider recommended follow-up actions, though this item was designated for discussion only without formal action.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for a property at 4104 Shorey Avenue, which would require demolition of an existing structure.

discussions.item: Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider an intergovernmental agreement related to Military Road construction, involving coordination with other governmental entities on road infrastructure.

discussions.item: Construction Services Proposals for Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project
discussions.body: The Board was set to review and potentially select construction services proposals for improvements to the Alderson and Jelinek intersection.

discussions.item: Construction Services Proposals for Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider construction services proposals for the reconstruction of Bloedel Avenue, a significant road infrastructure project.

The Board was scheduled to consider renewal of multiple business licenses for the 2026-2027 term, including adult establishment, tobacco, lodging, alcohol, and salvage licenses through the consent agenda process.

publicComment: Public comment was scheduled for non-agenda items with a three-minute limit per speaker. Additional public comment opportunity was included as part of the public hearing on park and recreation impact fees.

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Public Hearing
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed significant increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. The proposed changes would raise single-family fees from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the Village's 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

The Board was expected to consider a resolution expressing support for a long-term, sustainable funding solution to Wisconsin's Transportation Fund. This resolution was set to address state-level transportation infrastructure funding concerns.

discussions.item: Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to review and potentially act on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. This represents a significant public safety infrastructure project for the community.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was set to discuss feedback on the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and recommended follow-up actions. This item was designated for discussion only with no action expected.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for the property at 4104 Shorey Avenue. Raze orders typically require demolition of structures deemed unsafe or in violation of building codes.

The Board was scheduled to consider Ordinance No. 26-008 amending Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding Mobile Food Vendors License requirements. This was set to update licensing procedures for food truck and mobile vendor operations.

The Board was expected to review and act on construction services proposals for two major infrastructure projects: the Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project and the Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project.

discussions.item: Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider an intergovernmental agreement related to Military Road construction, which typically involves coordination with neighboring municipalities or county government on shared roadway projects.

The Board was set to discuss potential interest in completing CliftonStrengths assessments and training, a professional development tool designed to help identify individual strengths and improve team dynamics.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda, allowing persons to address the Board for up to three minutes regarding non-agenda items, with time extensions permitted at the Chief Presiding Officer's discretion. Additional public comment opportunity was scheduled during the Park and Recreation Impact Fees public hearing.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

Approve the Minutes

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Wausau Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: Wausau City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
